The  grammar of more complex clauses and sentences is covered separately with the structures used in questions, negatives, imperatives and The  main points are summarised in 215. Other entries in this Section deal English has fairly fixed word order, especially in formal writing and speech.

What do you take me for? (= ‘ I’ m  not stupid.’  - interrogative for statement) Your coat's on the floor. (= 'Pick up your coat.' - declarative for instruction) Structure and meaning don’ t always go together.

What a good idea! How optimistic you are!

•    exclamatory clauses: typically used (naturally) for exclaiming Try cutting it a bit shorter. Write to your MP  about it.

•    imperative clauses: typically used for giving instructions, making Does my hair look OK?  What haven't they done this time?

•     interrogative clauses: typically used for asking questions Your hair looks nice. The government haven 't done it.

•    declarative clauses: typically used for making statements (positive or of structure: Simple (one-clause) sentences come  in four main types from the point of view INTRODUCTION Section 19  Basic Clause Types grammar •  Section 19   Basic Clause Types 225   let introducing imperatives hope, seem, etc                                    224   imperatives 219    negative structures with think,   223    exclamations 218    negative questions                               ever, yet, etc 217    negative structures: basic rules       222   non-affɪrmative words: anybody, 216    questions: basic rules                      221    ambiguous negatives word order                                               see nobody.

I was playing badly. I’ll see you at the club on Tuesday.

The children had probably gone home.
